title: My Sister and I Rode the Bus Line
summary: Interviewed by Joe Mosnier in Atlanta in April 2011, Jamila Jones opens with her Montgomery upbringing by her mother and grandmother alongside her sister Doris and a younger brother, and how she and Doris, riding the Montgomery bus line to school, experienced the driver's daily harassment that drew them toward the bus boycott.

title: She Taught Us We Were Important
topic: Rosa Parks and the Youth Council
summary: Jones recalls her sister introducing her to Rosa Parks through the NAACP Youth Council, where Parks read from National Office papers and taught the young people that they mattered, that they should learn their rights, that voting was vital, and that they should carry themselves responsibly, then served them Kool-Aid afterward.

title: The 381 Days, We Walked
summary: Jones describes a youth movement that organized itself to cross town once the buses were abandoned, her sister refusing rides so as not to take an adult's space, walking the full 381 days, and the joy at the boycott's end when riders no longer had to take the abuse she had endured on the bus since age six.

title: The White Lady With the Red Hair
summary: Riding from the first stop, Jones tested sitting at the front and had to give up her seat for one red-haired white woman she never forgot, and recounts reaching up to touch the woman's hair as if by accident to learn what made her different, concluding the hair was no different and resolving to walk however long it took.

title: We Practiced Freedom Songs
summary: Jones tells how she and other girls at the Alabama State Laboratory High School formed a group to compete in a Friday talent show and, though about Diana Ross's age, chose freedom songs, so that when the boycott came they were ready and were invited to sing at the teas held all over Montgomery to raise money for the movement, naming Gladys Carter and Minnie Hendrix among the four.

title: Reverend Seay Each Sunday
topic: Churches and a High School Influence
summary: Jones explains the girls sang mostly at school at first because they attended different churches, hers being Mount Zion AME Zion, and names Reverend Solomon Seay, who held the first meeting for the bus boycott and spoke each Sunday about the community's needs, as a formative influence on her commitment to the freedom struggle through her high school years to 1962.

title: The Montgomery Gospel Trio
topic: Highlander, New York, and a Name
summary: Jones recounts being selected for a cultural exchange at the Highlander Folk School, where she met people from around the world including Africa, and traveling to New York with Reverend Sheldon's help to record one of the first albums of civil rights fundraising songs, an occasion that finally forced the unnamed group, once just called the girls, to choose the name Montgomery Gospel Trio.

part: The Freedom Riders Come to Montgomery
title: That Day We Stole a Car
summary: Jones recalls that the adults' wounds from the movement left the youth to act, so when James Bevel and Bernard LaFayette arrived with the Freedom Riders and Juanita Abernathy had no one to fetch them, the young people borrowed Antoinette Carson's grandfather's car, chalk-marked its tires to return it exactly, and went to pick up the riders, witnessing how they had been beaten getting off the buses.

part: The Freedom Riders Come to Montgomery
title: We're Going to Do Like the Doodlebug
summary: Jones reflects that youthful fear never outweighed the need to participate, and describes the night the Freedom Riders reached the mass meeting and the church was surrounded by armed white men on horses, where Reverend Solomon Seay, who called her doodlebug, answered her fear with the children's doodlebug song about a house on fire, a meaning she grasped only as an adult, and they spent the night inside.

title: Let's Continue to Sing
summary: Jones clarifies she was not in the original Freedom Singers, keeping the Montgomery Gospel Trio going through Alabama State while singing voter-registration meetings for James Bevel's group in rural areas, then coming to Atlanta to work with Bernice Johnson Reagon on the Pinpoint Festival, after which she and Mattie Casey of the Harambee Singers knocked on Reagon's door asking to keep singing and started the Harambee Singers.

title: This Little Light of Mine
summary: Jones explains that what they sang depended on the moment and the message, that they learned at Highlander a song could carry the movement's message anywhere, and names This Little Light of Mine, We Are Soldiers in the Army, and Old Freedom among the songs they performed at eleven and twelve years old that later became the basis of the movement's repertoire, with Jones singing melody and much of the lead.
keywords: We Are Soldiers in the Army

title: Ain't Gonna Ride No Buses No More
summary: Jones credits a professor at Alabama State with writing the first song for the Montgomery movement, describes the group as traditional singers who at first only added a word or line, and tells how, after seeing others rework songs at Highlander with Guy Carawan, they began writing their own, including a reworked traditional song whose pointed line asked whether the white folks knew they would not ride the buses anymore.

title: Do You Have to Sing So Loud
topic: The We Are Not Afraid Verse
summary: Jones recounts traveling to Highlander by night at fourteen on Reverend Seay's advice, and the night police cut the town's power to plunge the workshop into darkness, when someone began the We Are Not Afraid verse of We Shall Overcome and the whole room joined, growing louder until a visibly shaken policeman asked her not to sing so loud, the moment she understood the power of the music as the voices blended in the dark.

title: That Verse Steadied My Knees
summary: Jones describes a later march to the Montgomery capitol, leaving from King's church when he had returned for a campaign, and facing bare-chested white men with rifles on horses at the top of the steps, where with her knees shaking she sang the We Are Not Afraid verse again and it steadied her.

title: Twenty-Seven Page Questionnaires
summary: Jones recounts her college years working voter registration and sit-ins with James Bevel and James Orange of SCLC, venturing beyond a tightly watched Montgomery, learning nonviolence training to protect oneself when spat upon, and going back to age twelve when Rosa Parks and Mrs. Williams taught the youth in a hairdresser's shop to fill out long questionnaires and recite the Preamble so they could teach adults to register.

title: Hands Off Nkrumah
summary: Jones explains that by the founding of the Harambee Singers around 1966 she had turned her attention to Black Power and the Pan-African movement, so the group wore Afros and sang radical international songs that no longer included the Montgomery trio's repertoire, among them Hands Off Nkrumah, which named the CIA amid the upheaval in Ghana.

title: Sitting Under the Table
summary: After a break, Jones turns to the community that shaped her, recalling Flora Grant of Atlanta Life Insurance who kept close to movement news and talked with her mother as Jones listened from under the card table, the woman who wrote See How They Run, the early Black jockey Sullivan Rogers, and her storytelling aunt she called Besidee.

title: Pry, Boy
topic: A Sharecropper's Tale and a Song
summary: Jones retells her aunt Besidee's story of a sharecropper called John who, unable to feed his family despite working from sun up to sun down, taught his young son to pry up the white man's chicken coop so he could take a single chicken, the boy trembling as he held it, a tale that so unsettled her she later wrote a song from it, and she closes that such stories and songs taught the young what their choices would make of their lives and keep her, at sixty-seven, still fighting for freedom.
